observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator comply strictly with the requirements of Sections 2.1 and 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 on the submission of the required list of on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year as well as the posting of signboards to facilitate monitoring and validation of the Municipality’s awarded and implemented projects.

We recommended that BAC Chairperson direct the BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report of the Municipality and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester in accordance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and furnish the Audit Team a printed copy of the PMR to provide basis for monitoring the status of procurement activities of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Management require the contractor to submit regularly the Statement of Work Accomplished/Progress/Final Billing for on-going and completed projects as of year-end;
Municipal Engineer furnish the Municipal Accountant with the Project Status Report together with the Statement of Work Accomplished/ Progress/Final billing for partially completed/on-going and completed projects as of year-end indicating their percentage of completion and cost incurred to date, which shall be the basis of the latter in recording the Construction in Progress or reclassification of CIP to appropriate PPE accounts upon completion, to ensure complete, accurate and timely recognition of assets in the financial statements; and
Municipal Accountant to draw journal entry to compute and record in the financial statements the related depreciation of these assets reckoned from the date of their completion.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Account to:
Ensure that the necessary disclosures, as stipulated under Paragraph 54 of International Public Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 3, are adequately presented in the Notes to Financial Statements; and
Comply with the criteria, particularly on the keeping of accounts, and adequacy of report presentation as mandated under Sections 111 and 121 of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) conduct an investigation and reconciliation on the noted discrepancy amounting to ₱2.585 million; and (ii) record the necessary adjusting entry to correct the amount of Trust Liabilities - DRRM account, which should be in agreement with the amount reported in the SAAOB, RAAO and bank statement balance maintained for the unexpended DRRM fund;
Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the required subsidiary ledger for the unexpended DRRM balance for ease of monitoring of the receipts, disbursements, and unexpended balances of the Special Trust Fund by year of transfer; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately transfer actual cash pertaining to the unexpended balance of DRRM accruing to the STF for CYs 2023 and 2024 totaling ₱3.179 million pursuant to Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts under the three funds and book the necessary adjustments to bring their balances into agreement. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should closely monitor transactions affecting reciprocal accounts and ensure that these are recorded in the different funds simultaneously to avoid any error or misstatements in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Record the adjustment to bring the Advances to Contractor account to its accurate balance; and
Ensure that all accounts and financial transactions are recorded in accordance with their nature/timing of their occurrence to safeguard against inaccurate or misleading information pursuant to Section 111 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The GSO to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and conduct immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties through its disposal committee in accordance with Section 79 of PD 1445, to prevent further deterioration and loss of additional income;
The GSO to update the IIRUP after disposal of unserviceable properties and furnish a copy thereof to the Municipal Accountant and Audit Team; and
Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant draw a journal entry voucher to derecognize/drop from the books of accounts the disposed unserviceable properties based on the IIRUP submitted by the GSO.

We recommended and the Management agreed to comply with the following requirements moving forward:
The GSO prepare the required RIS and RRSI for all issued relief goods, supplies and materials which should be submitted to the Accounting Unit for the recording of appropriate expense;
The GSO and the requisitioning department prepare and submit: (i) list of beneficiaries indicating the quantity expressed in terms of kilogram/sack distributed and the signature of recipients signifying their acceptance/acknowledgement of the relief/welfare goods; and (ii) photographs taken during distribution of said items to the beneficiaries; and
The Municipal Accountant record the expense only upon receipt of RIS and RRSI from the GSO and other supporting documents.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Local Chief Executive to: (i) abide with the provisions of Civil Service Commission (CSC) Memorandum Circular No. 21, s. of 1991 and Rule XVII of the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of Executive Order (EO) No. 292; and (ii) ensure that all department heads enforce policies as regards time and attendance system; (ii) consider upgrading the biometric system into a one which can directly generate DTRs of individual employees following the format of CSC Form No. 48;
Department heads to: (i) ensure that all employees under their supervision have a work schedule recorded in the timekeeping system; (ii) maintain a separate logbook in respective departments to record time-ins and outs and leaves of employees and submit photocopies of these at the end of each pay period to the HRMO to serve as ther check and balance; (iii) ensure that approved leave forms are submitted to the HRMO and a copy retained after submission thereto; (iv) review all DTRs for inconsistencies and ensure correctness of time and attendance of each employee before signing/approving such DTRs; and
Human Resource Management Office to: (i) consider installing an alternative mechanism/system for recording time and attendance of employees deployed in the field such as the use of logbook and ensure monitoring whether these employees abide with the timekeeping policies and are really rendering actual services; and (ii) develop and enforce concrete policies for those employees who fail to abide with the policies concerning time and attendance and filing of leaves of absences.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Human Resource and Management Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Stop assigning job order personnel in performing collection function and issuance of accountable forms. Instead, designate revenue collector who is a qualified regular employee; and
Reassign the concerned job order personnel to perform tasks other than handling of cash and issuing official receipts, or assign duties not performed by regular employees in compliance with CSC Resolution No. 02-0790 dated June 5, 2002.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GSO to cause the immediate marking of the 25 units of motor vehicles with the word “For Official Use Only” (3 inches) and 19 units bearing the name of the agency operating or using the same as required under COA Circular No. 75-6 and Manual on the Audit of Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed, as head of the GAD Focal Point System, in coordination with the GAD Focal Person to:
Monitor compliance in the formulation and maintenance of the GAD Agenda, Code and Database. These institutional mechanisms are fundamental in the furtherance of the LGU’s GAD efforts in gender mainstreaming in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01. Also, observe deadlines in the submission of the GAD AR, complete with essential attachments, to facilitate proper evaluation and assessment of the implementation of the GAD PPAs; and
Maximize the utilization of GAD funds through full implementation of GAD-related PPAs in order to attain the objectives for which the funds are provided.

We appreciate the effort of Management in the prompt remittance of the current contributions and loan amortizations due the GSIS and for complying strictly with the provisions of Republic Act No. 8291. Notwithstanding, we recommended and Management agreed to require the Accounting Office to:
Perform the necessary reconciliation with the GSIS to trace the unremitted balances amounting to ₱0.853 million which have accrued for several years, for proper disposition;
Improve monitoring systems to ensure that all contributions and loans withheld during the period are remitted intact moving forward; and
Maintain subsidiary ledgers to keep track of contributions and loan deductions withheld and remitted per individual employee.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant and the Accounting staff in charge of Pag-IBIG remittances to: (a) observe full remittance of fund contributions and loan payments deducted from employees’ compensation to avoid imposition of penalties and surcharges for late and incomplete remittances; and (b) trace and remit in full the unremitted balances of ₱1.082 million.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort to trace the previous years’ balance of ₱51,598.06 and cause its immediate remittance to avoid the accumulation of discrepancies. Further, maintain and review postings to the respective subsidiary ledgers (SLs) and reconcile/adjust the differences immediately.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following courses of actions:

The Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Inventory Committee to complete physical inventory of all PPE items, prepare the updated RPCPPE and a copy thereof shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of the following year;

The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to set up the correct depreciation of the Wheeled Excavator Equipment recorded under the Construction and Heavy Equipment account reckoned from the date of acquisition up to the present by debiting prior period adjustment and crediting accumulated depreciation and ensure that all depreciable PPEs are provided with depreciation pursuant to PPSAS 17; and

The Municipal Accountant effect the reclassification of semi-expendable properties below the capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00 to their appropriate expense accounts and record the cumulative effect of change in accounting policy using prior period adjustment and government accounts, as prescribed under Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to undertake a detailed reconciliation/analysis of the accounts and investigate causes of the error/discrepancies between the GL and the financial statements for the purpose of booking up the necessary adjustments to arrive at the correct balances of the accounts; and

Require the Accounting Office to maintain subsidiary ledgers (SL) for easy monitoring/tracing of accounts and a periodic reconciliation to their respective general ledger (GL) balances or controlling accounts shall be made at the end of every month to avoid existence of negative/abnormal balances.

The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reclassify the costs of major repairs to their appropriate PPE accounts, and compute for the corresponding depreciation taking due consideration of the change in the estimated useful life of the assets as a result of major repairs, if warranted.

The Municipal Accountant limit the use of account “Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses” (5-02-99-990) for expenses not otherwise classified under the specific maintenance and other operating expenses accounts and record the actual expenses based on the accurate classification of financial transactions as documented and supported by accounting records pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Unit (LGUs) as described in Annex B, Section 5.2 of Commission on Audit Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

Immediately send demand letters to officials and employees, who have outstanding cash advances as of December 31, 2023, to settle the overdue amounts within a specific period. Otherwise, effect the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 until such time that cash advances are fully settled; and

Direct the designated AOs to strictly comply with the rules and regulations particularly on the liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed period to ensure that all disbursements are recorded in the books in the appropriate time/period.

Determine first the objectives to be achieved in incurring certain expenditures, identify the factors affecting the expenditures, be it price or any established limits and find alternatives or ways to reduce expenditures to minimum without sacrificing the attainment of the objectives; and

Ensure that only reasonable expenditures are incurred and strictly follow the maximum allowable Daily Travelling Expenses of government personnel as prescribed under EO No. 77.

Payments of salaries and other allowances of all personnel of the Municipality be strictly and properly supported with the printed Daily Time Records coming from the biometric machine of the Municipality, duly verified by the employee’s immediate supervisor. No salary of contractual, job order, casual, co-terminus or permanent employee should be paid without the required Daily Time Record.

The Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the concerned department heads to prepare and cause the immediate submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) for COA AAR for CY 2022 using the attached prescribed format, henceforth observe strict compliance to COA Memorandum No. 2014-002 dated March 18, 2014.

Payments for goods and services delivered as well as other operating expenses be made directly to the suppliers pursuant to the provisions of Section 93 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. The Municipal Treasurer limit the payments through reimbursement or cash advance and the Municipal Accountant deduct/withhold the mandatory taxes pursuant to Section 4.114.2(a) of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 13-2018 dated March 15, 2018.

Management, through the BAC, ensure that all procurements should be meticulously and judiciously planned in accordance with the approved APP and should pass through the regular mode of procurement, as possible, to promote competitive prices pursuant to RA No. 9184.

Instruct the BAC to use the prescribe format of the Annual Procurement Plan and ensure that vital information are provided thereto in accordance with (GBBP) Resolution No. 20-2015 dated July 16, 2015; and

Direct the BAC to undertake procurement in bulk to save time, costs and manpower hours using public bidding as the primary mode of procurement and discontinue the practice of procuring supplies and equipment through shopping or small value procurement unless the conditions in Section 52.1 and 53.9 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 are met.

Management Comment:
Management admitted that while they exceed the maximum allowable daily travelling expense of ₱1,800.00 per day, some expenses have been dispensed with such as venue rentals and other incidental expenses which could cost more to the LGU if paid separately.
Auditor’s rejoinder:
Auditor rejoined that expenses incurred are costly on the part of the LGU which is over and above the maximum allowable daily travelling expenses of ₱1,800.00 per day. Nevertheless, alternative ways should have been sought such as the location of the training venues which is more economical without sacrificing the attainment of the objectives.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the coordination between the Municipal Accountant and the designated General Services Officer towards the full implementation of the perpetual inventory method in the recording of all Inventory Accounts to ensure proper accounting and promote better control in the receipt and issuance of supplies and materials; and
The Municipal Accountant and designated General Services Officer maintain the prescribed ledger cards and stock cards, respectively, for each type of Inventory, keep track the actual Supplies Inventory on hand and reconcile the balances of the two inventory records.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Instruct the BAC to use the prescribe format of the Annual Procurement Plan and ensure that vital information are provided thereto in accordance with (GBBP) Resolution No. 20-2015 dated July 16, 2015; and
Direct the BAC to undertake procurement in bulk to save time, costs and manpower hours using public bidding as the primary mode of procurement and discontinue the practice of procuring supplies and equipment through shopping or small value procurement unless the conditions in Section 52.1 and 53.9 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 are met.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Engineer to: (i) fast track the application and processing of electrical and water connections for the project to become fully operational and to serve the very purpose for which it was implemented; and (ii) facilitate the immediate repair and replacement of damaged or stolen items in the facility;
Assign personnel to conduct routine maintenance and inspection activities in order to prevent undue deterioration and damage to the facility; and
Install safeguard mechanisms to the project such as fence or grills and security locks, if warranted, to protect government properties from loss and unauthorized use.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal DRRM Council to prepare and submit the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Investment Plan (LDRRMIP) indicating under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years for ease of monitoring of project implementation and evaluation;
The MDRRMC and Municipal Budget Officer ensure that the LDRRMF be strictly utilized for the implementation of projects and activities for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation, and recovery; and
The Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting entry to record the transfer of current year’s unexpended QRF and DRRM-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund totaling ₱2.119 million in order to reflect the correct balance of Trust Liabilities-DRRM account in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the concerned Accountable Officers to settle their cash advances pursuant to COA Circular no. 97-002, especially at year-end, for the timely recognition of expenses in the books of accounts; and
Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances by sending demand letters regularly and withhold the payment of any money due them in case of their failure to settle the liquidation of cash advances despite demand.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the NGOs/POs and the Municipal Accountant to submit to Commission on Audit a Status Report on the Implementation of the Project and a satisfactory written explanation as to why liquidations of funds transferred to NGOs/POs remained outstanding in the books of accounts over the last three years;
Demand from the NGOs/POs to return the unutilized fund/excess amount of funds to the Municipality; and
Ensure that succeeding fund releases to NGOs/POs strictly adhere to the rules and regulations embodied in COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and 2012-001.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments to recognize a liability representing the unutilized balance of the BGCM fund amounting to ₱40,167.75 and maintain a separate subsidiary ledger in compliance with LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020; The Municipal Accountant and Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit the required documents to the Office of the Auditor for further evaluation of the transactions pursuant to Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020; Require the Municipal Budget Officer/Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the monthly report on fund utilization and status of implementation of PPAs and post the accumulated reports on the LGU's website, if any and in at least three conspicuous public places in the locality at the end of every month; and Require the BAC to strictly comply with the posting requirement of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 for information purposes and to promote transparency and accountability in the conduct of Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act.

Submit the following documentary requirements: Accomplishment Report showing computations of actual accomplishments as of September 30, 2020, of at least an average of 70 percent based on the total number of programs/activities/projects actually implemented and delivered as against the total number of programs/activities/projects targeted to be implemented in the approved LGU budget for FY 2020, excluding those targets/outputs critically affected by the imposed restrictions/limitations due to COVID-19; Documents showing identifications/listings of cost-cutting and systems improvement measures and justifications on how the available balances of MOOE allotments was realized or generated as a result of such measures; and Observe strict compliance with the guidelines in the grant of Collective Negotiation Agreement (CNA) Incentive for succeeding years pursuant to DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-5 dated November 4, 2020 and subsequent issuances pertaining thereto.

The Municipal Accountant see to it that payments of honoraria to BAC Members, BAC Secretariat and TWG is duly supported with the documentary requirements enumerated in 5.7.1 of COA Circular No 2012-001 in order to establish the validity and correctness of the claims; and
The absences incurred by the members of the BAC, BAC Secretariat and TWG be considered in the computation of their respective honoraria proportionately based on the meetings attended during the procurement process.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team the documentary requirements as required under COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 27, 2007 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for evaluation and review; and
Ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 27, 2007 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in releasing government funds to NGOs/POs.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to properly recognize fund transfers already made to NGOs/POs by debiting Due from NGOs/POs (1-03-06-330) and a credit to Membership Dues and Contributions to Organization (5-02-99-060);
Management require the implementing NGOs/POs to submit Fund Utilization Report on the Implementation of the funds received from the Municipal Government; and
Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the proper recording and classification of the financial transactions in the financial statements of the local government unit as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the establishment of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other information and to coordinate with the Municipal GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and the Local DILG Field Officer on the generation and processing of the data in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01; and
Facilitate the immediate hiring of GAD Database Encoder to ensure the effective implementation of the Municipal GAD Database that contains systematically gathered and regularly updated information relevant to gender responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the BAC Chairman require the BAC Secretariat to post the Notices of Award and Notices to Proceed together with the approved contracts for the procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and services in the PhilGEPS and agency website, if there is any, within the prescribed period as required under Section 37.1.6 and Section 37.4.2 of the IRR of RA 9184.

Recommendation:
We recommended that BAC Chairperson direct the BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report of the Municipality and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester in accordance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and furnish the Audit Team a printed copy of the PMR to provide basis for monitoring the status of procurement activities of the Municipality.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management encourage the JO and contractual personnel to register as SSS self-employed members in adherence to the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-83 to avail of the benefits and protection against hazards and disability, sickness, maternity, old age, death and other contingencies that the SSS offers.

We recommended that management expedite the implementation of the specific projects on solid waste management program to ensure the comprehensive coverage for the protection of the public health and environment in the municipality. 

We further recommended that all projects embodied in the Annual Investment/Development plan shall be implemented in the year it was appropriated so as not to deprive the constituents of the benefits that can be derived from the implementation of these projects.

We recommended that Liquidation Reports for cash advances for the Supplemental Feeding Program be supported with daily attendance sheets and weighing results of children beneficiaries before the start of the feeding program and every month thereafter until completion of the 120 feeding days to establish the propriety of claim on the feeding program and to be able to properly evaluate improvement and sustenance in the nutritional status of children beneficiaries.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Accountant in coordination with the MSWD Office commented that they will submit the required attendance sheets and weighing results of children beneficiaries before the commencement of the feeding and every month thereafter so as to monitor the progress of beneficiaries and effectivity of the program.

We recommended the following:
The management should submit the following documents:
CSC-approved PRAISE as bases for the grant of the monetary award;
Records or data that incentives and awards to each employee was based on performance, innovative ideas and exemplary behavior;
Information or records that monetary awards was granted only when the suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments and other personal efforts result in monetary savings and it does not exceed 20% of the savings generated; and
Agency’s annual Work and Financial Plan and budget showing at least 5% of the HRD Funds allocated for the PRAISE is incorporated.
Management should carefully evaluate the validity of giving out incentives, especially the basis thereof and whether those granted the incentives are qualified to receive the same; and
See to it that all disbursements is supported with complete documentation in adherence to Section 4 (6) of P.D. 1445.
Management’s Comment:
The management commented that they will comply with the documentary requirements as prescribed in Civil Service Commission (CSC) Resolution No. 010112 and Memorandum Circular No. 01, Series of 2001 and abide with the existing rules and regulations on the grant and payment of other allowances and benefits.

We recommended that:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) through the General Services Office shall implement control over the use of government vehicle through properly accomplished and duly approved and serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets, a summary of which shall be made at the end of each month in a Monthly Report of Official Travels for audit purposes; and
Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels shall likewise be prepared and submitted to the Office of the Auditor for verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of the fuel consumed during the period.
Management’s Comment:
The management commented that they will supply the Driver’s Trip Ticket with vital/complete information and submit the required Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels to the Office of the Auditor in compliance with COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977, prescribing the use of the “Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles”.

We recommended that management require the liquidation of cash advances within the time frame required by law. The Municipal Accountant should stop the practice of granting additional cash advances unless the previous cash advances were fully liquidated.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advances by sending demand letters regularly and withhold the payment of any money due them in case of their failure to settle the liquidation of cash advances despite demand. Likewise, the management institute appropriate measures/actions to enforce liquidations of cash advances in accordance with the afore-cited rules and regulations.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Accountant claimed that the cause of material amount of unliquidated cash advance appearing in the records was due to cash advances for intelligence and confidential fund by the former mayor and the Numancia PNP in which liquidation documents were already submitted to COA Central Office for audit and settlement. However, they were advised that there are still lacking documents to be complied and they are in the process of gathering the required documents for its immediate settlement.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team the documentary requirements as required under COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 27, 2007 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 for evaluation and review; and
Ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 27, 2007 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in releasing government funds to NGOs/POs.
Management’s Comment:
The management assured the Audit Team that they will comply with the documents required as enumerated in COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to properly recognize fund transfers already made to NGOs/POs by debiting Due to NGOs/POs (1-03-06-330) and a credit to Trust Liabilities (2-04-01-010);
Management require the implementing NGOs/POs to submit Fund Utilization Report on the Implementation of the funds received from the Municipal Government ; and
Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the proper recording and classification of the financial transactions in the financial statements of the local government unit as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Accountant commented that the necessary adjustments will be made in CY 2018 to properly recognize fund transfers already made to NGOs/POs in adherence to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015. Moreover, the corresponding Fund Utilization Report will be required from the implementing NGO/PO.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the establishment of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other information and to coordinate with the Municipal GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and the Local DILG Field Officer on the generation and processing of the data in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01; and
Ensure the effective implementation of the Municipal GAD Database that contains systematically gathered and regularly updated information relevant to gender responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.
Management’s Comment:
The management commented that they have already a base data manually available from each department which will help the establishment of systematic GAD database. Moreover, the LGU has already procured 2 units IT equipment in the later part of CY 2017 intended for the establishment of GAD database system and with the integration of Information Technology and manpower specifically assigned to update and maintain GAD related information, the LGU expects that GAD related issues and concerns can now be effectively and efficiently addressed starting CY 2018.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to promptly prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within fourteen (14) calendar days after end of each semester in accordance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and furnish the Audit Team a printed copy of the PMR to provide basis for monitoring the status of procurement activities of the Municipality.
Management’s Comment:
The management commented that a personnel will be assigned to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and furnish the Audit Team a printed copy of the PMR.

We recommended that the collectors/accountable officers to prepare and submit monthly the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) to the Municipal Treasurer who in turn shall consolidate the reports in the Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) and submit these reports to the Office of the Audit Team Leader not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month in order to facilitate monitoring and inventory of accountable forms.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Treasurer commented that the Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) is being prepared monthly, however, the same was not submitted to the Office of the Auditor. They only submit accountable forms attached to the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD). The Municipal Treasurer assured the Audit Team that they will submit the required report within the reglementary period in accordance with the regulations.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal Development Council and the budget committee during the preparation of annual budget for 20% Development Fund to include only Programs/Projects/Activities which are feasible and could be accomplished within the target date of completion, after taking into consideration the priorities of the LGU leadership.
Implement the development projects programmed during the year as embodied in the Annual Investment/Development Plan and ensure optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund in compliance with the provisions of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 for the benefit of the constituents of the Municipality;
The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), the Municipal Engineer, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant closely monitor the implementation of the different development programs, projects and activities as reflected in the Annual Investment/Development Plan and reconcile the reports submitted to the Audit Team;
Implement immediately all the projects under the continuing appropriation; and
At the end of each year, review all unimplemented projects and verify whether they can be implemented in the succeeding year. All savings from completed projects and all projects which are no longer feasible and cannot be implemented should be considered for immediate realignment, subject to budgetary rules or reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% Development Fund for re-appropriation to other development projects.
Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Government commented that the causes of accumulation of delayed/unimplemented projects were due to various projects downloaded by National Government Agencies (NGAs) thru KALAHI, BUB and ADM which were implemented within the time frame set by the NGAs and lack of manpower to oversee and supervise implementation of various projects.
The BAC also commented that those unimplemented projects have been scheduled for public bidding in the 1st quarter of CY 2018.

We recommended that:
The LDRRMO review and update annually the LDDRMF Investment Plan to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund; and
Municipality strengthen the capabilities of its Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to enable it to perform its mandated functions under Section 4, Rule 6, IRR of R.A. 10121 especially in the formulation of an LDRRM Plan/LDRRM Investment Plan as guide in the utilization the LDRRMF and proper monitoring of the fund.
Management’s Comment:
The LDRRM Officer commented that those unimplemented projects were already considered for realignment and utilize the same to purchase rescue vehicle and other DRRM-related projects in order to enhance the disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the LGU.

We recommended that management expedite the implementation of the projects programmed for the implementation of the solid waste management program to ensure the protection of the public health and environment in the municipality.
We further recommended that all projects embodied in the Annual Investment/Development plan shall be implemented in the year it was appropriated so as not to deprive the constituents of the benefits that can be derived from the implementation of these projects.
Management’s Comment:
The management commented that a site was already identified for the establishment of LGU dumpsite and already met with the land owner to negotiate with the terms. However, it was not pushed through as the land owner disagreed with the purpose of its utilization.
The management further commented that they are still in the process of finding site for the establishment of dump site so that Solid Waste Management programs and projects could be implemented to ensure the protection of the public health and environment.

We recommended that Management ensure that at least one percent of its Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) be allocated in order to ensure implementation of programs, projects and activities for the strengthening of the Local Councils for the Protection of Children (LCPC).
Management’s Comment:
The management promised to appropriate the minimum amount equivalent to one percent of its IRA for the succeeding years so that adequate funds could be utilized for the implementation of programs, projects and activities for the strengthening of the LCPC and ensure that rules, regulations and guidelines thereof are strictly followed by the LGU.
